Conclusion: 

A well-defined user and role concept is a key success factor for operating SAP Integration Suite securely and efficiently across all landscape stages. While SAP’s standard role collections already cover most common use cases, custom role collections allow organizations to implement a clearer separation of duties, comply with audit requirements, and follow the principle of least privilege.

Feel free to use the comment section or reach out to us via SAP Integration Services.

---

Modernize. Integrate. Innovate.

entry.title: ATUL Architecture #5 — Designing a Utility Data Fabric on SAP BTP
entry.content:
ATUL Architecture #5 — Designing a Utility Data Fabric on SAP BTP

How Utilities Build a Unified, Governed, Real‑Time Data Layer for Grid + Customer Operations

1.      Introduction

For SAP-centric utilities, the challenge is no longer simply managing growing volumes of data. Utilities have invested heavily in SAP IS-U, SAP S/4HANA, AMI platforms, SCADA systems, GIS applications, outage management solutions, and a growing ecosystem of grid technologies. Yet much of this information remains trapped within operational silos, making it difficult to create a unified view of grid and customer operations.

The real problem is no longer collecting data, but connecting, governing, and using it across the enterprise.

As utilities navigate the rise of distributed energy resources (DERs), electric vehicles (EVs), advanced metering infrastructure (AMI), and increasingly dynamic grid operations, traditional batch-oriented architectures struggle to keep pace. The grid is becoming real time, but the data architecture often remains fragmented.

A Business Data Fabric built on SAP Datasphere and SAP HANA Cloud provides a modern architectural approach for unifying SAP and non-SAP data without discarding existing investments. By combining data integration, semantic modeling, governance, and real-time access capabilities, utilities can create a trusted enterprise data layer that supports analytics, AI, operational intelligence, and regulatory reporting from a common foundation.

This is where Utility Data Fabric becomes essential—and where SAP BTP provides the platform to make it practical.

Most utilities still operate with:

  • Siloed operational systems
  • Fragmented data marts
  • Batch‑driven ETL pipelines
  • Inconsistent semantic definitions
  • Limited real‑time visibility

This creates a fundamental gap: The grid is real‑time, but the data architecture is not.

This is where Utility Data Fabric becomes essential — and SAP BTP provides the platform to build it.

2.      A Utility Data Fabric in Action: Transformer Outage Scenario

To understand how Utility Data Fabric works, consider a common operational event transformer failure affecting several hundred customers.

A SCADA system detects abnormal voltage conditions and reports the event in real time. AMI smart meters simultaneously stop communicating, confirming the outage footprint. GIS identifies the affected transformer, feeder, and service territory, while the Outage Management System (OMS) creates an outage event and initiates restoration activities.

Within the Utility Data Fabric, these events are combined with asset history, maintenance records, weather conditions, and customer information to create a unified operational view. Customer notification systems can automatically communicate outage information, analytics platforms can assess business impact, and AI models can predict restoration timelines based on historical outage patterns.

This single event touches nearly every layer of the Data Fabric—from data ingestion and semantic modeling to governance, analytics, and AI-driven decision support.

3.      What Utility Data Fabric Actually Means

Data Fabric is not a single product. It is an architectural approach that unifies:

  • Data ingestion
  • Data storage
  • Semantic modeling
  • Governance
  • Access control
  • Analytics
  • AI/ML consumption

Across all utility domains:

  • Grid operations
  • Customer operations
  • Metering
  • Billing
  • Asset management
  • Field operations
  • Regulatory reporting

In simple terms: A Data Fabric makes data findable, usable, trusted, and real‑time — across the entire utility.

SAP BTP provides the building blocks to make this practical.

4.      The Core Components of a Utility Data Fabric on SAP BTP

Data Lake Layer

This is the foundation. SAP HANA Cloud provides the underlying multi-model data platform for storing and processing enterprise data, while SAP Datasphere delivers the semantic, integration, and governance capabilities that form the Business Data Fabric layer. A key principle of SAP's Business Data Fabric approach is that not all data must be physically moved into a central repository. SAP Datasphere supports both replication and federation techniques, allowing utilities to access and combine data across SAP and non-SAP systems while minimizing unnecessary data duplication. This approach helps reduce integration costs, improve data freshness, and preserve investments in existing operational platforms. For large historical datasets such as AMI interval reads and telemetry archives, SAP HANA Cloud Data Lake can provide lower-cost storage while maintaining integration with Datasphere and HANA Cloud analytics services. In the transformer outage scenario, telemetry from SCADA, outage events from OMS, AMI meter status, GIS network topology, and customer information are ingested and made available through the Data Fabric for unified analysis

The Data Fabric approach allows utilities to federate data across hybrid landscapes—keeping legacy data in on-premises systems while virtualizing access in the cloud—reducing the latency and cost of moving massive, static datasets.

Utilities ingest:

  • AMI meter events
  • SCADA/IoT streams
  • DER telemetry
  • Weather + Wildfire Risk Data
  • Customer interactions
  • Billing determinants
  • Asset health and maintenance history
  • Outage management events (OMS)

For utilities, geospatial information is as important as transactional data. Modern Data Fabrics must integrate GIS systems, network topology models, feeder maps, outage polygons, vegetation management zones, and wildfire risk areas. By combining spatial context with customer, asset, and operational data, utilities can perform advanced use cases such as outage impact analysis, restoration prioritization, vegetation risk assessment, DER hosting capacity planning, and climate resilience modeling. SAP Datasphere can combine this geospatial context with operational and customer data to create business-ready models for planning, analytics, and AI use cases.

  • The data lake must support:
  • High‑volume ingestion
  • Low‑cost storage
  • Real‑time streaming
  • Time‑series analytics
  • Geospatial analysis

This is where grid + customer data finally live together.

Analytical Model and Consumption Model concepts in Datasphere. In SAP Datasphere, analytical models define measures and semantics, while consumption models expose business‑ready views for analytics and AI. The goal is not to move every dataset into one database. The goal is to provide one trusted view of enterprise data regardless of where it physically resides

Semantic Models

This is the most misunderstood part of a Data Fabric.

Semantic models define:

  • What is a meter?
  • What is an outage?
  • What is a transformer?
  • What is billing determinant?
  • What is a customer event?

Without semantic consistency:

  • Analytics break
  • AI models fail
  • Reports contradict each other
  • Regulatory submissions become risky

A Business Data Fabric combines data integration, governance, semantic modeling, and real-time access into a single enterprise architecture without requiring all data to be physically centralized. SAP Datasphere’s Business Data Fabric capabilities allow utilities to create:

  • Harmonized data models
  • Domain‑specific views
  • Reusable data products
  • Cross‑domain relationships.

Rather than exposing raw tables or application-specific structures, utilities can publish governed data products that package data with business meaning, ownership, quality standards, and access controls. Examples include a customer 360 product, Asset Health product, Outage Operations product, DER Portfolio product, or Transformer Risk product. These reusable data products become the primary mechanism through which analytics teams, AI solutions, operational applications, and business users consume trusted enterprise data. For example, during a transformer outage, different systems may refer to the same asset using different identifiers. Semantic models help establish a consistent definition of transformers, feeders, outages, and affected customers across all participating systems.

Master Data Foundation

Semantic consistency begins with trusted master data. Utilities often maintain customer, asset, feeder, transformer, meter, and service point information across multiple systems, including SAP S/4HANA, SAP IS-U, GIS platforms, outage management systems, and third-party applications.

Utility Data Fabric must establish a consistent master data foundation so that the same business object is represented identically across the enterprise. Without master data harmonization, analytics become unreliable, AI models generate inconsistent results, and cross-domain reporting loses credibility.

By combining semantic models with governed master data, utilities create a trusted foundation for enterprise-wide analytics, operational intelligence, and regulatory reporting. SAP Master Data Governance (MDG) can further support this foundation by governing critical utility master data domains and synchronizing trust records across enterprise applications.

Governance Layer

Governance is not documentation. It is control + trust: Utilities must define

  • Data ownership (domain‑driven)
  • Data quality rules
  • Lineage and traceability
  • Access policies
  • Retention and compliance
  • Security and privacy

SAP BTP provides:

  • Central governance via Datasphere
  • Cataloging and lineage
  • Role‑based access
  • Policy enforcement
  • Auditability

Governance is what makes Data Fabric safe. Utility Data Fabrics must also address cybersecurity and regulatory requirements. As operational technology (OT) data from AMI, SCADA, and grid systems becomes integrated with enterprise IT platforms, utilities must enforce appropriate segmentation, access controls, auditability, and compliance requirements such as NERC CIP where applicable. During an outage investigation, data lineage and auditability help utilities understand where information originated, how it was transformed, and whether operational decisions were based on trusted data sources.

Active Metadata: The Intelligence Behind the Data Fabric

A modern Data Fabric does not operate solely on data; it also depends on metadata that describes how data is created, transformed, consumed, and governed. Active metadata captures information such as data lineage, ownership, quality scores, usage patterns, business definitions, and relationships between datasets.

Within SAP Datasphere, metadata helps utilities understand where data originated, who owns it, how it is being used, and whether it meets governance standards. As utility data landscapes continue to grow, active metadata becomes essential for automated impact analysis, policy enforcement, data discovery, and trusted AI consumption.

In many successful Data Fabric implementations, metadata acts as the operational intelligence layer that continuously monitors and improves the health of the overall data ecosystem.

Analytics for Grid + Customer

This is where Data Fabric becomes valuable. Utilities can build:

Grid Analytics

  • Outage prediction
  • Load forecasting
  • DER hosting capacity
  • Feeder‑level performance
  • Voltage optimization
  • Asset failure prediction

Customer Analytics

  • High‑bill alerts
  • Usage pattern segmentation
  • EV charging behavior
  • Payment risk scoring
  • Real‑time notifications
  • Personalized rate recommendations

Enterprise Analytics

  • Regulatory reporting
  • Operational KPIs
  • Financial performance
  • Workforce optimization

SAP Analytics Cloud + Datasphere + HANA Cloud provide a unified analytics stack.

AI Readiness Through Data Fabric

The long-term value of a Utility Data Fabric extends beyond reporting and analytics. It provides the trusted data foundation required for enterprise AI, machine learning, and autonomous decision-making. AI models perform best when they are trained and operated on governed, semantically consistent, and high-quality data.

By combining semantic models, master data harmonization, governance controls, and lineage capabilities, a Utility Data Fabric reduces the risk of inaccurate predictions, inconsistent recommendations, and model drift. This foundation becomes critical for initiatives such as outage prediction, asset failure forecasting, energy demand optimization, and intelligent customer engagement. Examples include outage prediction, transformer failure forecasting, DER optimization, and proactive customer notification models. In the transformer outage example, AI models can combine outage history, asset condition, weather data, and crew availability to improve restoration estimates and recommend remediation actions.

BTP Integration:

This architecture doesn't exist in a vacuum. It integrates directly with SAP Build Process Automation (for triggering workflows from data insights) and SAP AI Core (for deploying models trained on Datasphere data products). SAP Integration Suite plays a critical role in Data Fabric architecture by connecting SAP IS-U, SAP S/4HANA, SAP EAM, GIS systems, outage management platforms, and external data providers. Through Cloud Integration, API Management, and Event Mesh, Integration Suite enables secure, governed, and scalable movement of data across hybrid utility landscapes.

How the Data Fabric Works End‑to‑End

Step 1 — Ingest

  • AMI events via Event Mesh
  • IoT/SCADA via MQTT
  • Customer interactions via APIs
  • Asset data via S/4HANA

Traditional data architectures rely on batch-based movement of information between systems. Modern utility operations require a different approach. Smart meters, distributed energy resources, grid sensors, and customer engagement platforms continuously generate events that must be processed in near real time. SAP Event Mesh enables these events to be distributed across operational systems and data platforms, where they can be consumed by analytics, automation services, and AI models. creating a continuously updated Data Fabric rather than a periodically refreshed repository.

Step 2 — Store

  • Raw zone (immutable)
  • Refined zone (cleaned)
  • Curated zone (business‑ready)

Step 3 — Model

  • Semantic definitions
  • Domain views
  • Data products

Step 4 — Govern

  • Quality rules
  • Lineage
  • Access control

Step 5 — Consume

  • Grid operations
  • Customer operations
  • Field mobility
  • AI/ML models
  • Analytics dashboards

This is the Utility Data Fabric lifecycle.  The Data Fabric helps align grid‑level latency requirements (millisecond responses for protection) with enterprise‑level latency (near real‑time analytics), ensuring the data architecture does not become a bottleneck for critical operations.

Returning to our transformer outage example, the same operational event moves through every stage of the Data Fabric lifecycle—from ingestion and storage through semantic modeling, governance, analytics, and AI-driven decision support.

5.       Why Utilities Need Data Fabric Now

  • Because the grid is becoming real‑time: DERs, EVs, and AMI require continuous intelligence.
  • Because AI depends on clean, governed data: AI is useless without trusted data.
  • Because regulators expect transparency: Lineage, auditability, and data quality are no longer optional.
  • Because customer expectations have changed: Real‑time notifications, usage insights, and personalized experiences require unified data.
  • Because operational silos are too expensive: A Data Fabric reduces duplication, integration cost, and data chaos.

6.      Data Fabric vs Traditional Utility Data Warehouse

Many utilities already operate enterprise data warehouses and may question why a Data Fabric is needed. Traditional data warehouses typically rely on periodic ETL processes that copy data into centralized repositories for reporting purposes. While effective for historical analytics, they often struggle to support real-time operational use cases, cross-domain intelligence, and AI-driven decision making.

Utility Data Fabric complements and extends the traditional warehouse by introducing semantic consistency, federated data access, active governance, reusable data products, and near real-time integration. Rather than simply storing data, the Data Fabric creates a governed and intelligent data layer that connects operational systems, analytical platforms, and AI services across the enterprise.

In simple terms, a traditional warehouse answers questions about the past, while a Data Fabric enables utilities to act on what is happening now.

Traditional Data Warehouse

  • Periodic ETL
  • Historical reporting
  • Centralized storage
  • Primarily analytics focused

Data Fabric

  • Real-time data access
  • Federation and virtualization
  • Semantic consistency
  • Data products
  • AI consumption
  • Cross-domain intelligence

7.      Common Pitfalls (What Fails in Real Projects):

Pitfall: Attempting to replicate all data instead of federating it. Leverage Datasphere’s ability to virtualize data from SAP S/4HANA and external sources to reduce ETL overhead and latency.

  • Treating the Data Fabric as an IT project: It must be business‑owned and domain‑driven.
  • Overloading the data lake with unstructured noise: Raw data without governance becomes a “data swamp.”
  • No semantic consistency: Different teams define the same metric differently.
  • Ignoring real‑time ingestion: Batch pipelines cannot support modern grid operations.
  • Not designing data products:  Data must be reusable, not one‑off.

The Architecture Principle That Matters Most

Data Fabric is not a database. It is a governed operating model for how utility data is shared, trusted, and consumed across the enterprise.

SAP BTP provides the platform.  The utility provides discipline.

What Success Looks Like

A successful Utility Data Fabric delivers:

  • A single source of truth
  • Real‑time grid + customer visibility
  • Faster regulatory reporting
  • Higher data quality
  • Better AI outcomes
  • Lower integration cost
  • Stronger cybersecurity posture
  • Improved customer experience

This is the foundation for the AI‑enabled utility.

8.      Executive Insight

Historically, utilities integrated applications.

The next generation utility will compete on the quality, trustworthiness, and usability of its data as much as the reliability of its physical infrastructure.

Organizations that establish trusted, governed, and reusable data products will be better positioned to deploy AI, automate operations, accelerate regulatory reporting, and improve customer experience. The competitive advantage will come not from possessing more data, but from creating a data architecture capable of turning data into operational intelligence.

9.      Final Thought

Utilities don’t need more data. They need better architecture for the data they already have.

A Utility Data Fabric on SAP BTP creates:

  • A unified data layer
  • A governed semantic foundation
  • A real‑time operational backbone
  • A scalable platform for AI and analytics

In the next decade, utilities that master their data will lead. Those that don’t will struggle with reliability, cost, and customer expectations.

Next in the Series — ATUL Architecture #6

We move into the intelligence layer:

  • AI‑driven grid operations
  • Predictive models for BTP
  • Real‑time decision engines
  • Digital twins
  • Autonomous workflows

This is where Data Fabric becomes the brain of the modern utility.

entry.title: Model Context Protocol (MCP) : From APIs to AI-Ready Interfaces
entry.content:
Introduction

For decades, APIs have been the foundation of digital integration. An application needs customer information. It calls an API. A mobile application needs to create an order. It calls an API. A business process needs to retrieve an invoice, update a record, or trigger an action. Again, it calls an API. This model works extremely well because traditional software is deterministic. The developer knows which API to call, which endpoint to use, which parameters to send, and how to process the response.

Then large language models and AI agents entered the picture.

An AI agent does not necessarily begin with an endpoint, an operation, and a predefined sequence of calls. It may begin with a goal:

“Find my meetings with this customer, retrieve the latest support issues, summarize the current situation, and prepare a follow-up.”

To complete this task, the AI application must determine:

  • Which systems are relevant?
  • What capabilities are available?
  • Which capability should be used first?
  • What information is required as input?
  • How should the result of one operation be used in the next?

This creates a different integration problem. The question is no longer only:

How can one application call another application?

It is increasingly:

How can an AI application understand what capabilities are available and use them appropriately?

This is the problem space addressed by the Model Context Protocol, or MCP.

What is the Model Context Protocol?

MCP is an open protocol that standardizes how AI applications connect to external capabilities and contextual information. It provides a common interaction model between an AI application and the systems it needs to work with.

These systems may provide access to:

  • business applications;
  • enterprise data;
  • documents and files;
  • databases;
  • search systems;
  • development tools;
  • internal services;
  • existing APIs.

The key idea is simple:

Instead of teaching every AI application separately how to interact with every system, MCP defines a common protocol through which available capabilities can be discovered and used.

 An MCP Server can describe what it offers, what inputs are required, and how those capabilities can be invoked. An MCP-compatible application can discover this information through the protocol. This makes MCP particularly relevant for AI agents. An agent may not know in advance every capability it will need. It may need to inspect what is available, reason about which capability is relevant to the current task, invoke it, evaluate the result, and continue with another action.

MCP provides a stanzdardized foundation for this interaction.

Why Do We Need MCP When We Already Have APIs?

This is usually the first question. The answer begins with an important distinction:

APIs and MCP are designed for different interaction models.

An API is primarily a contract between software systems. Consider an API operation such as:

GET /customers/{customerId}/orders

The developer integrating with this API typically needs to know:

  • where the API is hosted;
  • which operation to call;
  • how authentication works;
  • which parameters are required;
  • what request format is expected;
  • what response structure will be returned;
  • how errors should be handled.

The application logic is then explicitly programmed around this contract. This is an excellent model for deterministic software. AI agents introduce another requirement. They need more than the ability to execute an operation. They need enough structured information to reason about available capabilities.

For example:

  • What can I do?
  • Which capability is relevant to the current goal?
  • What information does it require?
  • When should I use it?
  • What should I do with the result?

This is where MCP differs from a conventional API interaction. An MCP Server does not merely provide an address that can be called. It exposes capabilities in a standardized, machine-readable form that an AI application can discover and understand.

A useful way to express the distinction is:

An API tells software how to interact with a system. MCP helps an AI application understand what capabilities are available for it to use.

 

 

APIs Assume Prior Knowledge

Traditional API integrations usually begin with documentation. A developer reads the API specification, understands the available operations, selects the required endpoint, writes the integration logic, and deploys the application. The consuming application itself does not normally discover the business purpose of an endpoint and decide whether it should use it. That decision has already been encoded by the developer.

The flow typically looks like this:

Developer understands API → Developer writes integration logic → Application executes predefined API calls

With MCP, the interaction can be different:

MCP Server describes capabilities → AI application discovers them → Model reasons about relevance → Capability is invoked

This shift is fundamental. The knowledge about available capabilities is no longer buried entirely inside application code, integration logic, or a long system prompt. It can be exposed through a standard protocol.

A Simple Example

Imagine an AI assistant responsible for preparing a customer meeting.

The user asks:

“Prepare me for tomorrow’s meeting with AKS Corporation.”

To complete the task, the assistant may need to:

  1. find the meeting in the calendar;
  2. identify the participants;
  3. retrieve recent customer interactions;
  4. find open support cases;
  5. retrieve recent orders;
  6. summarize the information.

Using traditional APIs

The application developer may need to integrate separately with:

  • a calendar API;
  • a CRM API;
  • a support system API;
  • an order management API.

For every integration, the developer must understand the API contract, implement authentication, map requests and responses, handle errors, and explicitly define how the AI application should use the integration. The application may also require detailed tool definitions or instructions explaining when and how each operation should be called.

Using MCP

Each relevant system can expose capabilities through an MCP Server.

For example:

  • list_upcoming_meetings
  • get_customer_details
  • find_open_support_cases
  • get_recent_orders

The AI application can discover these capabilities and their input definitions.

It can then reason:

  • I first need to identify tomorrow’s meeting.
  • The meeting information gives me the customer.
  • I can use the customer identifier to retrieve support cases and orders.
  • I can combine the results into a meeting brief.

The underlying systems may still use REST APIs, OData services, SOAP services, databases, or other technologies. The AI application does not necessarily need to understand all of those implementation details. It interacts through the MCP interface.

The MCP Architecture

At a conceptual level, an MCP-based interaction involves three important roles.

MCP Host

The host is the AI application in which the user interacts with the model.

It may be:

  • an AI assistant;
  • an agentic application;
  • a developer tool;
  • an enterprise AI application.

The host manages the overall user experience and coordinates the AI model's interaction with external capabilities.

MCP Client

The MCP Client maintains the protocol-level connection to an MCP Server. It communicates with the server, discovers what the server offers, and enables the host application to use those capabilities.

MCP Server

The MCP Server exposes capabilities and context through the MCP standard. It acts as the bridge between the AI application and an external system.

That external system may be:

  • an enterprise application;
  • an API;
  • a database;
  • a file system;
  • a document repository;
  • an internal business service.

The conceptual flow is:

User → AI Application / MCP Host → MCP Client → MCP Server → Enterprise System

The MCP Server is therefore not the AI model itself. It is the standardized interface through which AI applications gain access to external capabilities.

 

 

What Can an MCP Server Expose?

MCP defines several primitives for interaction. Three of the most important are Tools, Resources, and Prompts.

Tools: Actions the AI Application Can Perform

Tools represent executable capabilities.

Examples include:

  • create a purchase order;
  • search for a customer;
  • retrieve open incidents;
  • calculate a shipping estimate;
  • create a support ticket;
  • update a business record.

A tool typically describes:

  • its name;
  • what it does;
  • the inputs it expects.

This information helps the AI application determine whether the tool is relevant to the task. The key point is that a tool is not merely an endpoint. It is a capability presented in a form designed to be discovered and invoked in an AI-oriented interaction.

Resources: Context the AI Application Can Access

Resources provide access to information that may be useful as context.

Examples include:

  • documents;
  • files;
  • database records;
  • product information;
  • configuration data;
  • knowledge content.

The AI application can retrieve relevant information and use it as part of its reasoning process.

Prompts: Reusable Interaction Templates

Prompts provide reusable templates for common interactions. They can help standardize how particular tasks are initiated or how context is assembled for a model. Together, these primitives create a structured way for AI applications to interact with external systems.

MCP Does Not Replace APIs

The phrase “MCP versus APIs” can create the wrong impression.

MCP is not a replacement for APIs. APIs remain fundamental to enterprise architecture. They provide reliable access to business systems, data, and operations. In many implementations, an MCP Server will invoke existing APIs behind the scenes.

The architecture may look like this:

AI Application → MCP Server → Managed API → Business System

The API continues to provide the business capability. The MCP Server exposes that capability in a way that an AI application can discover and use through a standardized protocol. 

A more accurate comparison is therefore:

APIs expose systems to software. MCP exposes capabilities to AI applications.

They can work together.

An organization that has already invested in well-designed and well-managed APIs is not starting again. Those APIs can become the foundation for AI-ready capabilities exposed through MCP.

Why Standardization Matters

Without a common protocol, every AI integration risks becoming a custom adapter. One model expects tools in one format. Another framework uses a different schema. A third application requires its own connector model. The result is repeated integration work. MCP introduces a common protocol boundary. An MCP-compatible client can interact with an MCP-compatible server using the same fundamental protocol concepts.

This creates the possibility of a broader ecosystem in which AI applications can connect to capabilities without every combination requiring a completely new integration model. The value is not that custom work disappears.

Enterprise systems will still require:

  • authentication;
  • authorization;
  • business mapping;
  • error handling;
  • lifecycle management;
  • monitoring;
  • governance.

The value is that the model-facing interaction layer becomes standardized.

The Enterprise Question: Connectivity Is Not Enough

Making a capability available to an AI agent is only the beginning.

In an enterprise environment, several additional questions immediately arise:

  • Who is allowed to expose a capability?
  • Which AI applications are allowed to discover it?
  • Which users are authorized to invoke it?
  • How is access authenticated?
  • How are sensitive operations protected?
  • How is usage monitored?
  • How are changes versioned?
  • How are capabilities governed throughout their lifecycle?
  • How can existing enterprise APIs be reused instead of rebuilt?

These concerns become even more important when AI agents can perform actions rather than merely retrieve information. A tool that reads product information has a different risk profile from a tool that creates a payment, updates a customer record, or deletes business data. This means the next phase of MCP adoption in enterprises is not simply about creating more MCP Servers.

It is about managing them as enterprise-grade integration assets.

Why MCP Matters for Integration Platforms

MCP creates a natural evolution for integration and API management platforms.

Enterprises already have valuable business capabilities exposed through:

  • REST APIs;
  • OData services;
  • backend applications;
  • API Proxies
  • API Compositions.

The opportunity is to make selected capabilities available to AI applications without duplicating the underlying business logic.

This raises several architectural requirements:

  • reuse existing enterprise APIs;
  • expose AI-oriented tools;
  • describe capabilities clearly;
  • manage authentication and authorization;
  • apply governance;
  • monitor execution;
  • control lifecycle and changes.

This is where the worlds of API Management, enterprise integration, and AI agents begin to converge.

APIs Built the Connected Enterprise. MCP Can Make It AI-Accessible.

APIs transformed enterprise architecture by giving applications standardized access to systems and data. MCP addresses the next challenge: giving AI applications a standardized way to discover and use capabilities.

The relationship can be summarized simply:

APIs provide access to business capabilities. MCP makes selected capabilities understandable and usable in an AI-oriented interaction model.

The future is therefore unlikely to be APIs or MCP. It is more likely to be:

APIs as the enterprise foundation, with MCP as an AI-facing capability layer.

For organizations that already have mature API landscapes, this is an important distinction. The move toward agentic AI does not invalidate existing API investments. It increases their strategic importance. The next challenge is to determine how those APIs and enterprise services can be exposed as well-designed, governed, and reusable MCP capabilities.

That is where this series begins.

Coming Next in This Series

In the next article, we will move from protocol fundamentals to enterprise implementation and explore MCP Server on Integration Cell in SAP Integration Suite.

We will examine how to seamlessly create an MCP Server on SAP Integration Suite, publish and consume it through Developer Hub, understand how MCP Servers fit into the broader integration landscape, and explore how organizations can move from simply exposing APIs to enabling governed interactions for AI agents.

entry.title: Cutover control for SAP Cloud Integration: CSV driven bulk shutdown and wave based start of iFlows
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION

I am working on a large ECC to S/4HANA migration where the SAP Cloud Integration tenant stays the same .Existing iFlows are repointed to S/4HANA rather than rebuilt. The cutover therefore requires interfaces talking to ECC to be stopped before data migration, repointed, and brought back up in a controlled sequence. Crucially, only the iFlows touching the migrating SAP system need to stop; the same tenant runs third party and non-SAP interfaces that must keep running. So "undeploy everything" is not an option . What is needed is selective undeploy and deploy of a defined list and this is exactly what this tool does.

On SAP PI/PO this was a solved problem: PIMON let you filter, multi-select and stop/start channels centrally, with status visible in the same place. CPI has no equivalent . The web UI handles one artifact at a time, which in a cutover window is slow and error prone. The model also differs: PI stopped the channel while the scenario stayed in place; in CPI the lever is undeploying the artifact from the runtime — design untouched, but stopping means removing the deployed artifact.

REQUIREMENT


The case for a tool comes down to two numbers — time and error rate. At real cutover scale, say 250 iFlows in scope:

  1. Reduce time. Manually, each undeploy is filter, select, click Undeploy, confirm, verify — 30 to 45 seconds per iFlow on a responsive tenant. That is close to 3 hours of pure clicking for 250 iFlows, and with scope cross-checking, evidence capture and interruptions, realistically 4 to 6 hours, usually split across several people — which adds its own coordination risk of items falling between the splits. The tool executes the same 250 undeploys via the API in under 10 minutes.

  2. Reduce error rate. Human accuracy on a repetitive 250-item selection task over hours is not 100%. Even a 1–2% slip rate means 2 to 5 wrong outcomes: an in-scope iFlow missed and still pumping messages into ECC during data migration, or an out-of-scope one undeployed — taking down a live interface that had nothing to do with the cutover. Worst of all, manual misses are silent. With the tool, the scope is a reviewed CSV and the follow-up -status run is a complete, deterministic audit of all 250 — not a memory of what got clicked.

    This blog describes a command line tool I built to solve this .The setup is:

    CSV list of iFlows --> Java CLI tool --> CPI OData API (v1) --> CPI runtime

    During cutover we needed:

    1. A reviewed and signed off list of in-scope iFlows

    2. A status check before the shutdown window (everything STARTED)

    3. A bulk undeploy of exactly that list

    4. A status check after (everything gone from runtime

    5. Console output that can be pasted into the cutover log as evidence

Only the interfaces in scope should be touched. Other integrations on the tenant keep running.


THE TOOL

The tool is a single jar Java 21 application taking three arguments:

java -jar cpi-iflow-controller-1.1.0.jar <config-file> <iflows-csv> <-status|-undeploy|-deploy>

The iFlow list is a two column CSV — package and iFlow name:

Package,iFlow Name
PACKAGEMONTHLYEXTRACTINTERFACES,MONTHLY_TAX_DATA
PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS,OTC_BILLING_DATA
PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS,OTC_MASTER_DATA
PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS,OTC_FI_DATA


Note the Package column contains technical package Ids.

When the cutover task says "stop in-scope interfaces", the definition of in-scope is this file.

 

GOTCHA : Package name and Package Id are not the same thing


This one cost me a debugging session. I first populated the CSV from the Manage Integration Content monitor, where every deployed iFlow shows a Package field:

The first run failed for every iFlow with "iFlow not found in package" — even though the monitor showed all of them as Started:




The reason: the Package field in the monitor is the package display name, but the OData API keys on the technical package Id — and the two differ. The Id is auto-generated from the name with special characters (including underscores) stripped, so a package named PACKAGE_OTC_OBJECTS has the Id PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS:



The same applies to artifacts — an iFlow has a display name and a technical Id, and the API wants the Id. In my case the two matched (the monitor shows both, under Name and ID), but do not assume they do.

Where to find the technical Ids:

  • Open the package in Design — the Package ID is on the Overview tab and in the browser URL (.../shell/design/contentpackage/<Id>)

  • Or query the API directly:

curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \

"https://<tmn-host>/api/v1/IntegrationPackages?$select=Id,Name&$format=json"

With the technical Ids in the CSV, the same run succeeds:

One more useful behaviour: for -status the package column can be left empty. The runtime API only needs the iFlow Id, so the tool skips package resolution and queries IntegrationRuntimeArtifacts directly:

Rule of thumb: the CSV takes technical Ids, not the display names in the monitor. For status-only runs the package column is optional; for deploy it must be correct, because the design-time lookup depends on it.

CHECKING STATUS

java -jar target/cpi-iflow-controller-1.1.0.jar config.properties iflows.csv -status

This prints the runtime status of each iFlow in the CSV:

+---------+---------------------------------+------------------+
| STATUS | PACKAGE | IFLOW |
+---------+---------------------------------+------------------+
| STARTED | PACKAGEMONTHLYEXTRACTINTERFACES | MONTHLY_TAX_DATA |
| STARTED | PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS | OTC_BILLING_DATA |
| STARTED | PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS | OTC_MASTER_DATA |
| STARTED | PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS | OTC_FI_DATA |
+---------+---------------------------------+------------------+
Summary: 4 succeeded, 0 failed (4 total)

Status is run at two points in the cutover: before the shutdown window to confirm the baseline (everything STARTED), and after the undeploy to confirm the interfaces are down. The two captures become the before/after evidence for the runbook verification task. Beyond cutover, the same command doubles as a quick health check across a defined interface list — after a tenant update or during hypercare. A useful side effect: it surfaces artifacts already in ERROR state, which you want to find before the cutover window, not during it.

UNDEPLOYING

java -jar target/cpi-iflow-controller-1.1.0.jar config.properties iflows.csv -undeploy

This walks the same CSV and undeploys each artifact via the OData v1 API (IntegrationRuntimeArtifacts). The summary line shows immediately if anything failed.
An important detail: undeploy is asynchronous in CPI. The OData call only submits the request — the API accepts it and returns, while removal from the runtime node happens in the background. A successful call means "the stop was requested", not "the interface is down". If an iFlow is mid-message, the runtime winds that down first. This is visible in Manage Integration Content right after the bulk undeploy — all four iFlows sit in Stopping state.

This is exactly why status matters in the shutdown sequence. After -undeploy, run -status against the same CSV; if some artifacts still show up, wait and re-run until the list is clear. The evidence that satisfies the verification task is the status output showing the interfaces are down — not the undeploy summary showing requests were submitted. The undeploy is the trigger; the status check is the confirmation.

Configuration:

Connectivity uses a properties file for the credentials

cpi.base.url=https://your-tenant.it-cpi018.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com
cpi.auth.type=oauth2
cpi.oauth.token.url=https://your-tenant.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
cpi.oauth.client.id=your-client-id
cpi.oauth.client.secret=your-client-secret


One config file per tenant: the same CSV is exercised against test during cutover rehearsal, then replayed against production by changing a single argument.

BTP service instance, plan and roles

The service key comes from a Process Integration Runtime instance with the api plan (not *integration-flow*, which is only for calling deployed iFlows). Create the instance in the BTP cockpit under Services > Service Marketplace, select the roles at creation, and create a service key with grant type client_credentials. The clientid, clientsecret, tokenurl and url map directly to config.properties above.

Three roles are needed, mapped to what each mode does:

Role

Used for

MonitoringDataRead

-status — reading runtime artifact status via IntegrationRuntimeArtifacts

WorkspacePackagesRead

Resolving iFlows inside a package at design time (IntegrationPackages / IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts)

WorkspaceArtifactsDeploy

-deploy and -undeploy — deploying and undeploying artifacts

These are set as parameters at instance creation. The exact configuration the tool has been verified against:

The token-validity of 43200 seconds gives a 12 hour token — longer than any cutover window, so the tool requests one token per run and never refreshes mid-sequence. Role names per API operation are listed in the SAP Help Portal under "Tasks and Permissions" for the Cloud Integration OData API.

A recommendation from experience: create a dedicated instance and key with only these roles, rather than reusing a broad integration developer key or an AuthGroup role — least privilege applies to cutover tooling too. For status-only runs (e.g. a hypercare health check), MonitoringDataRead and WorkspacePackagesRead are enough. And keep the filled-in config.properties out of Git — the repo commits templates only.

Controlled start: using deploy mode in waves

Deployment

The tool also supports a deploy mode:

java -jar target/cpi-iflow-controller-1.1.0.jar config.properties iflows-wave1.csv -deploy

However, shutdown and startup are not used the same way. In this migration the deploy step matters even more: between shutdown and startup the iFlows are repointed from ECC to S/4HANA — connection details, externalized parameters and endpoints updated — so each wave brings up the reconfigured interface, validated wave by wave against the new environment.

Shutdown is a blanket action — everything in scope must be down before migration starts, so one bulk undeploy of the full CSV is exactly right. Startup is a controlled start — interfaces return in a deliberate sequence, typically master data flows first and then transactional, gated by smoke tests and sign-offs between waves. Starting everything in one shot would flood a freshly migrated system with queued messages before anyone has validated the first interface.
At the same time, a wave is rarely a single interface — a master data wave might hold a dozen iFlows that come up together, and deploying those one by one through the UI has the same problem as the shutdown, just smaller. So the same CSV driven approach applies, with one CSV per wave:

Running: deploy with a wave CSV brings up all its iFlows in one command. Deploy is asynchronous like undeploy — the call submits, the runtime takes time to start — so the pattern repeats: -deploy the wave, then -status against the same CSV until everything shows STARTED, then run the smoke tests. Only after sign-off does the next wave go up.

The exercise on a trial 

I ran the full sequence on my tenant with the four iFlows split into two waves: all four stopped in one bulk undeploy (the Stopping screenshot above), then wave 1 (the three OTC iFlows) and wave 2 (MONTHLY_TAX_DATA) started separately.

Deploying wave 1 brings up all three OTC iFlows in one command — the DEPLOYING result confirms the requests were accepted

The asynchronous start is visible in the UI moments later — OTC_BILLING_DATA is already Started while the other two are still Starting:

Once the runtime finishes, -status against wave1.csv confirms all three STARTED — this is the check before wave 1 smoke tests:

 

Only then does wave 2 go up the same way:

Sequencing between waves stays a runbook decision. The tool automates everything within a wave — one command instead of a dozen clicks, with the status check confirming it.

GitHub Dev Containers

For this tool, and for development in general

The part I want to highlight is the environment. The classic failure mode of a utility like this: it works on the author's laptop, but the person executing the cutover task has JDK 17 instead of 21, or no Maven, or a proxy issue — and a five minute task becomes an hour of environment debugging inside the cutover window.

The repo ships with a .devcontainer definition: a small JSON file (plus optionally a Dockerfile) committed to the repo, describing the complete development environment — runtime versions, build tools, CLI utilities, VS Code extensions and settings. Open the repo and VS Code builds a container from it; your editor works inside that container.

How this differs from a traditional IDE setup

With a traditional IDE setup (Eclipse, IntelliJ, or plain VS Code on the host), the environment lives on the machine and only the project lives in the repo. JDK version, Maven settings, proxy config and plugins are per-machine state set up by hand, usually from a stale wiki page. Two people on the same project routinely have different environments, and nobody can prove which is correct.

With a dev container, the environment lives in the repo, next to the code:

  • The environment is versioned. When the tool moves to a newer Java version, the dev container moves in the same commit. Checking out an old tag gives you the old environment too — no traditional IDE setup offers that.

  • Onboarding is one click. Clone, "Reopen in Container", done. No install guide, no "which JDK" thread. Extensions and settings come along, so even the editor is consistent.

  • The host stays clean. Projects with conflicting toolchains (Java 17 here, Java 21 there, different Node versions for CAP projects) coexist without nvm/sdkman juggling — each project brings its own container.

  • It runs in the browser. GitHub Codespaces runs the same definition on a cloud VM with zero local install — genuinely useful when the person on cutover duty has a locked down laptop where installing a JDK needs a service ticket.

The trade-offs are small: Docker Desktop must be installed and licensed on the host (Codespaces avoids even this), the first build takes a few minutes, and Windows file I/O is slower unless the repo lives in WSL2. For most SAP side-car development these costs are negligible against the consistency gained.

This isn't unique to this tool. For a great walkthrough of the same pattern applied to SAP CAP development - showing how one dev container definition runs identically across local VS Code, GitHub Codespaces, and Business Application Studio - see DJ Adams' Developing CAP in containers – three ways.

Dev containers as a development practice, not just for this tool

I now treat the dev container as part of the deliverable for anything I build outside the ABAP stack, and it fits BTP development well — CAP (Node.js and Java), the supporting tooling that accumulates around a CPI workstream, and UI5/Fiori all benefit from pinned runtime and tooling versions, removing the "works for me" drift between developers entirely.

ABAP itself stays in the ABAP stack — but nearly everything around it in a modern SAP landscape is Java or Node, and all of it can be containerised this way.

For SAP integration teams this matters more than in mainstream web development. The team mixes ABAP developers, middleware specialists and functional consultants — you cannot assume a curated local Java setup. The dev container turns "build and run this Java tool" into a task anyone can execute the same way.

The cutover rehearsal workflow

Open the repo in Codespaces (or reopen locally in the dev container)

mvn clean package --> target/cpi-iflow-controller-1.1.0.jar
Copy the config and CSV templates; fill in tenant details and the signed off interface list

Run -status, capture, run -undeploy, run -status again, capture

Paste both tables into the cutover log

Conclusion

  • Cutover integration shutdown becomes deterministic with a CSV driven bulk undeploy instead of clicking through the CPI UI.
  • Package name and package Id are different — the monitor shows display names, the OData API wants technical Ids (auto-generated with special characters stripped). Populate the CSV with Ids.
  • The CSV doubles as the signed off shutdown scope; the before/after status tables double as cutover evidence.
    Undeploy is asynchronous in CPI — the call submits the stop request; the status check confirms the interfaces are down. Undeploy is the trigger, status is the evidence.
  • Ramp-up uses the same tool in deploy mode with wave specific CSVs — all iFlows in a wave deploy together in one command, while sequencing between waves (master data first, then transactional) stays a runbook decision gated by smoke tests and sign-offs.
  • A GitHub Dev Container means anyone can build and run the tool identically, including in the browser via Codespaces — and the same practice pays off for day to day CAP, CPI tooling and UI5 development, where the environment is versioned with the code instead of living per-machine. 

The code is on GitHub: https://github.com/viksingh/sap-cpi-iflow-controller

entry.title: Unlock value with SAP Integration Suite: Quarterly roadmap insights
entry.content:
New quarter. New innovations. New opportunities.

SAP Integration Suite continues to evolve, and every update creates new ways to help customers integrate systems faster, streamline business processes, and accelerate transformation.

Want the highlights without spending hours digging through roadmap updates?

Join our webinar, "Unlock value with SAP Integration Suite: Quarterly roadmap insights". We'll cover the biggest innovations from the past quarter, preview what's ahead, and dive into selected capabilities that every SAP Partner should know.

Whether you work in sales, presales, consulting, or delivery, these insights can help you turn roadmap updates into customer value and stronger engagements.

Don't miss your quarterly roadmap update.

Register now and stay ahead of what's next. https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/mar/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013385.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #SAPIntegrationSuite #Innovation #BusinessAI #DigitalTransformation

entry.title: SAP Event Mesh for Beginners – Part 2: Testing with Postman & CPI iFlow Integration
entry.content:
SAP Event Mesh for Beginners – Part 2: Testing with Postman & CPI iFlow Integration

Part 2 of a 4-part beginner series on SAP Event Mesh.

A quick recap

In Part 1, we covered the "why" and the "what" — Event-Driven Architecture, the publish-subscribe model, and the three building blocks of SAP Event Mesh: topics, queues, and namespaces. We then provisioned an Event Mesh instance on SAP BTP (region AP11), created queues for each consumer system, subscribed those queues to topics, and generated a service key holding our connection credentials.

👉If you haven't read it yet, start with Part 1: Event-Driven Architecture, Topics & Queues — this post builds directly on that setup. https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/sap-event-mesh-for-beginners-part-1-event-driven-architecture-topics-queues/bc-p/14428494#M179525 

So we have a working Event Mesh instance. But how do we actually send a message into it, and how do we get SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) to publish and consume events? That's what we'll do here.

We'll work in two stages:

  1. Validate the setup with Postman — prove that authentication works and that we can publish a message before we touch CPI.
  2. Build the CPI iFlows — one to publish events over HTTP, and one to consume them over AMQP.

Here's the picture we're building toward:

 

 

Let's get started.

3. Testing with Postman

Why test with Postman first? Because if something goes wrong later in CPI, we want to know the Event Mesh side is already working. Postman lets us isolate the problem. It's a habit worth building.

Step 1 — Configure OAuth2 authentication

Open Postman, create a new request, and go to the Authorization tab. We'll set it up like this:

Field Value
Auth TypeOAuth 2.0
Grant TypeClient Credentials
Access Token URLhttps://integrationsuite-<your-subdomain>.authentication.ap11.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
Client ID<uaa.clientid from your service key>
Client Secret<uaa.clientsecret from your service key>
Scope(leave empty)
Client AuthenticationSend client credentials in body

 

 

Now here's the single most common mistake people make, and it's worth pausing on:

⚠️Watch out: The Event Mesh Client ID contains special characters such as | and !. If we send the credentials as a Basic Auth header, those characters get corrupted during encoding, and we get a confusing invalid_client: Bad credentials error — even though our credentials are perfectly correct. The fix is to choose "Send client credentials in body." Always.

Click Get New Access Token. If everything is correct, Postman returns a token. We're authenticated. 🎉

Step 2 — Publish a message to a topic

Now let's send our first event. We use the messaging REST endpoint from the service key:

 
 
POST https://integrationsuite{}.cfapps.ap11.hana.ondemand.com     /messagingrest/v1/topics/{encoded-topic-name}/messages

Notice {encoded-topic-name}. This is where beginners lose an hour, so let's be careful.

The URL-encoding rule:

⚠️Every / in the topic name must be encoded as %2F — not %2. The trailing F is required. Leave it off and you'll get a 404 or, worse, silent wrong-topic routing.

So our topic name transforms like this:

  Value
Topic name (plain)S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify/CustomerMaster
Topic name (URL-encoded)S4HC%2FTesting%2FMasterdata%2FShopify%2FCustomerMaster

Which gives us the full URL:

 
 
https://integrationsuite {}-pubsub.cfapps.ap11.hana.ondemand.com/messagingrest/v1/topics/S4HC%2FTesting%2FMasterdata%2FShopify%2FCustomerMaster/messages

Next, set the headers:

Header Value
Content-Typeapplication/json
x-qos0

A quick note on x-qos (quality of service): 0 means at most once (fire and forget — fast, but no delivery guarantee), and 1 means at least once (guaranteed delivery). For our test, 0 is fine.

Finally, the request body — a simple CloudEvents-style payload:

 
 
json
{
  "specversion": "1.0",
  "type": "S4HC.Testing.Masterdata.Shopify.CustomerMaster",
  "source": "/S4HC/Testing/Masterdata",
  "data": {
    "CustomerID": "C001",
    "Name": "Test Customer"
  }
}

 

Hit Send. And now for the part that surprises everyone:

✅A successful publish returns HTTP 204 No Content. There's no response body, and that's correct. It does not mean something went wrong — it means the message was accepted and is now sitting in the queue, waiting for a consumer.

Step 3 — Verify the message arrived

Let's confirm. Go to the Event Mesh UI → Queues, click the queue you subscribed (for example S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify), and check the message count — it should have gone up by one.

 

That's it — we've published an event to Event Mesh and confirmed it landed in the right queue, all without touching CPI yet. Now we know the foundation is solid.

4. CPI iFlow Configuration

Time to bring CPI into the picture. We'll set up the shared credential once, then build the publish and consume sides.

4.1 Create the OAuth2 credential in Security Material

Both of our iFlows will authenticate the same way, so we create the credential once and reuse it.

In CPI, go to Monitor → Security Material → Add, and select OAuth2 Client Credentials:

Field Value
Credential NameEventMeshPOC (remember this name — we'll reference it in the adapters)
Grant TypeClient Credentials
Token Service URLhttps://integration-<your-subdomain>.authentication.ap11.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
Client ID<uaa.clientid from your service key>
Client Secret<uaa.clientsecret from your service key>
Send BodyYes

 

⚠️Same gotcha as Postman: make sure Send Body is set to Yes. The special characters in the Client ID will break Basic Auth.

4.2 Publishing messages — use the HTTP adapter

Here's an important design decision, and it catches a lot of people:

⚠️Known CPI behaviour: The AMQP adapter automatically lowercases topic and queue names. So CustomerMaster silently becomes customermaster, the topic doesn't match, and we get amqp:not-allowed (error 220041). To publish, we sidestep this entirely by using the HTTP Receiver adapter instead, which preserves the exact case.

In your publisher iFlow, add an HTTP Receiver adapter:

Connection tab

Field Value
URLhttps://enterprise-messaging-pubsub.cfapps.ap11.hana.ondemand.com/messagingrest/v1/topics/S4HC%2FTesting%2FMasterdata%2FShopify%2FCustomerMaster/messages
MethodPOST
AuthenticationOAuth2 Client Credentials
Credential NameEventMeshPOC
Content Typeapplication/json

Then, just before the HTTP adapter, add a Content Modifier step to set the required headers:

Header Name Value
Content-Typeapplication/json
x-qos0

 

That's the publish side done. When this iFlow runs, it POSTs the payload to the topic exactly the way our Postman test did — and we already know that works.

4.3 Consuming messages — use the AMQP adapter

When CPI is the consumer (the subscriber listening for events), we use the AMQP Sender adapter. And here's the key: we consume from a Queue, not a Topic — which conveniently avoids the lowercase issue we hit on the publish side.

AMQP Connection tab

Field Value
Hostenterprise-messaging-messaging-gateway.cfapps.ap11.hana.ondemand.com
Port443
Path/protocols/amqp10ws
Proxy TypeInternet
Connect with TLSChecked
AuthenticationOAuth2 Client Credentials
Credential NameEventMeshPOC

⚠️The Host field should contain only the hostname — no wss:// prefix. The protocol path goes in the Path field separately (/protocols/amqp10ws). Mixing them up gives errors like wss: Name or service not known or a failed WebSocket handshake.

AMQP Processing tab

Field Value
Destination TypeQueue
Queue NameS4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify
DeliveryPersistent

 

✅Tip: Using Queue as the destination type (rather than Topic) on the consume side keeps the exact name intact. Reading from a queue does not have the lowercase problem that publishing to a topic does.

Putting it together

Let's step back and look at what we've built:

  • We validated authentication and publishing with Postman, so we knew the Event Mesh side worked before writing a single iFlow.
  • On the publish side, our CPI iFlow uses the HTTP Receiver adapter to POST events to a topic — preserving case and avoiding the AMQP lowercase trap.
  • On the consume side, a second iFlow uses the AMQP Sender adapter pointed at a Queue to pull events as they arrive.
  • Both adapters share a single OAuth2 Client-Credentials credential, sent in the body.

The two recurring themes — case sensitivity and credentials in the body — are exactly the things that cause the most head-scratching for beginners, so keep them front of mind.

What's next

We now have a complete round trip: events flowing from CPI into Event Mesh and back out again. But the first time you wire this up, something will break — that's normal, and it's where real learning happens.

In Part 3 — Troubleshooting & Quick Reference, we'll go through the exact errors you're most likely to hit (invalid_client, amqp:not-allowed, the %2F trap, WebSocket handshake failures), what causes each one, and how to fix it — plus a one-page cheat sheet of every URL and value you need.

If this helped, a like or a comment is always appreciated — and feel free to share what you're integrating with Event Mesh. See you in Part 3!

 

Primary Tag: SAP Integration Suite Tags: SAP Event Mesh, Cloud Integration, SAP CPI, AMQP, OAuth2, Postman, SAP BTP, Event-Driven Architecture

entry.title: Lernen verbindet – Entdecke unsere 🇩🇪 deutschsprachigen 🇩🇪 Live Sessions in Q3
entry.content:
Q3 ist in vollem Gange. Welche Themen möchtest du dieses Quartal noch entdecken?

Vielleicht gibt es ein Thema, das schon länger auf deiner Lernliste steht. Vielleicht möchtest du dein Wissen vertiefen oder einfach neue Impulse mitnehmen.

Unsere deutschsprachigen SAP BTP Live Sessions bieten die Gelegenheit dazu. Von Grundlagen über Integration und Entwicklung bis hin zu Architektur- und Automatisierungsthemen wartet ein vielfältiges Lernangebot auf dich.

Vielleicht ist genau die Session, die dich weiterbringt, bereits Teil des aktuellen Programms:

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-platform-learning-group-blog-posts/new-sap-btp-live-sessions-auf-deutsch/ba-p/14415241

Also welches Thema möchtest du in Q3 noch entdecken?

Wir sehen uns in den Live Sessions!

entry.title: Building MCP Servers with SAP Integration Suite: What to Design, Not Just How to Connect
entry.content:
The MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite lets you expose any API as an MCP tool directly from API Management — giving AI agents governed, discoverable access to your systems, whether SAP or non-SAP. Getting the connection working is the easy part. The harder question is: what should your MCP tools actually look like?

What MCP adds on top of your APIs

Enterprise landscapes are rarely uniform. A single business process might touch an OData service in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, a REST API from Salesforce or ServiceNow, a SOAP service in a legacy SAP ECC system, and an RFC function module — each with different conventions and varying levels of documentation. For APIs that lack a proper spec, Integration Suite includes AI-assisted OpenAPI specification generation.

MCP provides a single, consistent interface on top of all of these:

  • Business intent — the tool description tells the agent what the operation means and when to use it
  • Multi-API orchestration — one tool can encapsulate calls across OData, REST, SOAP, and RFC — regardless of vendor
  • Governance — authentication, rate limiting, and audit logging applied consistently to every agent call
  • Broad system coverage — any system reachable via REST, OData, SOAP, RFC, or HTTP

Two ways to design your MCP tools — and why it matters

The endpoint-shaped approach (and its problems)

The natural first step is to expose existing endpoints as MCP tools one-to-one. This works for a demo, but creates problems in practice.

An agent is asked: "Create a new customer for Acme Corp, shipping address in Frankfurt, assigned to sales org DE01."

With endpoint-shaped tools:

Tool: get_business_partner_roles()
Tool: get_company_codes()
Tool: get_sales_organizations()
Tool: create_business_partner(name="Acme Corp", category="2")
Tool: create_business_partner_address(bp_id="...", city="Frankfurt", ...)
Tool: assign_business_partner_role(bp_id="...", role="FLCU01")
Tool: assign_customer_company(bp_id="...", company_code="DE01")
Tool: assign_customer_sales_area(bp_id="...", sales_org="DE01", ...)

Eight tool calls for one user request — each costing tokens and adding latency. The agent must track IDs across calls, reason about partial failures, and reconstruct SAP business logic on every run. If a business rule changes, every prompt that references it needs updating.

The intent-shaped approach

Tool: create_customer(
  name: "Acme Corp",
  city: "Frankfurt",
  country: "DE",
  sales_org: "DE01"
)

One call. The MCP server handles the sequence, enforces business rules, and manages error handling. The agent stays focused on the conversation.

What makes a good intent-shaped tool

  • Named after a business operation, not an API resource (approve_purchase_requisition, not patch_pr_item)
  • Business-meaningful parameters — accept sales_org: "DE01", not a raw ID the agent has to look up
  • Hides the sequence — the agent doesn't need to know that address creation must precede role assignment
  • Encodes business rules — the tool implementation handles logic the agent shouldn't need to reason about: which roles are mandatory, whether the requester has approval authority, what "overdue" means in your context
  • Returns a useful response — a confirmation the agent can relay, not a raw JSON dump

Tool descriptions matter

The tool description is what the agent reads to decide whether and how to use a tool.

Useful:

approve_purchase_requisition — Approves a purchase requisition in SAP S/4HANA. Use this when a user wants to approve a pending PR. Checks approval authority, updates the requisition status, adds the approval note, and advances the workflow to the purchasing team.

Not useful:

PATCH endpoint for purchase requisition status update.

How to build this with SAP Integration Suite

Joule / 3rd Party AI Agent
              ↓  MCP protocol
    MCP Gateway (SAP Integration Suite)
       ↓                  ↓                    ↓
  API Artifact       HTTP Endpoint       RFC Destination
  (OData/REST/SOAP)  (any HTTP service)  (S/4HANA / ECC RFCs)
       ↓
  optional: Cloud Integration iFlow
  (multi-system orchestration)
       ↓
  Any Backend (SAP, Salesforce, ServiceNow, legacy systems, ...)

Three creation paths are supported — choose based on your backend:

SourceWhen to use
Managed API artifact (OData / REST / SOAP)Any API already managed in API Management; add mediation steps inside the artifact if needed
HTTP endpoint + OpenAPI specAny external HTTP service
RFC destinationDirect access to SAP S/4HANA or ECC function modules

For multi-system orchestration, build a Cloud Integration iFlow, expose it as a managed API artifact, and create the MCP server from that.

Once the source is configured: select the operations to expose as tools, write a tool description for each, apply governance policies (authentication, rate limiting, audit logging), and publish to Developer Hub.

For step-by-step setup, see Design APIs and MCP Servers in the SAP Help Portal.

Before you publish: a quick checklist

  • Are your tools intent-shaped? If an agent needs to call three or more tools in sequence for a single user request, consider consolidating them.
  • Are business rules in the server, not the prompt? Sequencing logic and mandatory field rules belong in the MCP server, not in system prompts.
  • Do your tool descriptions explain intent? Write for an agent that understands business language, not for a developer reading API docs.
  • Is every call governed? Authentication, rate limiting, and audit logging should be applied at the API Management layer.
  • Do errors help the agent respond? Return "No business partner found with ID 1234567. Search by name using find_business_partner." — not { error: "PARTNER_NOT_FOUND", code: 404 }.
  • Are write operations clearly named? Tools that post documents, send emails, or trigger workflows should be named and described so the agent and user understand the consequences.

Summary

SAP Integration Suite gives you the complete stack: Cloud Integration for business logic and orchestration, API Management for governance, and the MCP Gateway to surface everything as MCP tools for any compatible AI client.

Build tools around what users want to accomplish — create_customer, approve_purchase_requisition, find_overdue_invoices — not around what endpoints exist. One well-designed tool that handles a complete business operation is more reliable, faster, and cheaper than a chain of granular API calls.
